stem: You are the obstetrics RMO assessing a 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 32 weeks gestation presenting with sudden onset painless bright red vaginal bleeding while at home. Her morphology scan at 20 weeks documented an anterior placenta covering the internal os. She reports no contractions, normal fetal movements, and a soaked maternity pad on the way in.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, no contractions, CTG reactive, no speculum yet performed
data_table.Investigations: Group and screen on file, FBC and coags being sent

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate step?

options.text: Avoid digital vaginal examination, admit for monitoring, and arrange ultrasound to assess placental location and fetal wellbeing
options.rationale: In suspected praevia, digital examination risks catastrophic haemorrhage; admission, monitoring, and ultrasound assessment are first-line. (Source: RANZCOG APH Statement)

options.text: Perform a gentle digital vaginal examination to assess cervical dilatation and determine the source of bleeding before arranging further imaging
options.rationale: Digital examination is contraindicated when praevia is suspected as it may precipitate massive haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG APH Statement)

options.text: Discharge home with safety netting and outpatient ultrasound in the next forty-eight hours once the patient is haemodynamically stable on observation
options.rationale: Antepartum haemorrhage from praevia requires inpatient observation and steroid administration; outpatient deferral is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG APH Statement)

options.text: Proceed to immediate category one caesarean section regardless of current haemodynamic stability or current cardiotocograph findings on assessment
options.rationale: Category one caesarean is reserved for ongoing massive haemorrhage or fetal compromise; assessment first is appropriate when stable. (Source: RANZCOG APH Statement)

options.text: Commence intravenous tocolysis with nifedipine to prevent preterm labour and continue conservative outpatient management following initial review
options.rationale: Tocolysis is not first-line for praevia bleeding and may mask deterioration; admission and monitoring are required. (Source: RANZCOG APH Statement)

explanation.summary: Painless bright red bleeding with a soft uterus and known praevia is classic for placenta praevia. Digital examination is contraindicated. Management is admission, IV access, group and crossmatch, antenatal corticosteroids if under 35 weeks, anti-D if Rh negative, CTG monitoring, and ultrasound to confirm position and exclude accreta. Definitive delivery is by elective caesarean at 36 to 37 weeks for stable cases.
key_points: Painless bleeding + known praevia = no digital exam
Anti-D 625 IU within 72 hours if Rh negative for sensitising event
explanation.pearls: RANZCOG places the elective caesarean window for major praevia at 36+0 to 37+0 weeks unless prior bleeds or accreta concerns prompt earlier delivery.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 34-year-old primigravid woman at 33 weeks gestation referred with worsening headache and visual scotomata over the last six hours. She has had right upper quadrant pain since this morning. BP at her last antenatal visit four days ago was 132/86. There has been no per-vaginal bleeding and the fetal movements are present.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/108 repeated 15 minutes later 162/106, HR 96, RR 18, SpO2 98%
data_table.Examination: Hyperreflexia with two-beat clonus, mild epigastric tenderness, no peripheral oedema, fundi sharp
data_table.Investigations: Urine protein:creatinine 86 mg/mmol, platelets 92 x10^9/L, ALT 138, AST 142, creatinine 78, urate 0.42, LDH 480
data_table.Medications: Aspirin 100 mg from 14 weeks, pregnancy multivitamin

lead_in: Which intervention has the strongest evidence for reducing maternal morbidity in this scenario?

options.text: Phenytoin loading dose intravenously for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia given its established efficacy in non-pregnant populations
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is superior to phenytoin for seizure prophylaxis in eclampsia. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Statement)

options.text: Intravenous hydralazine bolus titrated to a target systolic blood pressure under 130 mmHg before further evaluation or specialist input is obtained
options.rationale: BP target is around 140 to 150 systolic to avoid hypoperfusion; aiming under 130 risks placental compromise. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Statement)

options.text: Magnesium sulfate intravenous loading dose followed by infusion to reduce the risk of eclamptic seizures while delivery is being planned
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate halves the risk of eclampsia in severe pre-eclampsia (Magpie trial) and is the strongest evidence-based maternal intervention pending delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Statement)

options.text: Furosemide intravenous bolus to manage presumed fluid overload contributing to the rise in blood pressure during this preterm gestation
options.rationale: Furosemide is not standard in pre-eclampsia without pulmonary oedema; volume restriction is the focus. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Statement)

options.text: Aspirin dose increase to 300 mg daily for the duration of admission to optimise placental perfusion and reduce ongoing maternal morbidity
options.rationale: Aspirin is for prevention before disease, not treatment once severe features are present. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Statement)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia with neurological symptoms, hepatic involvement, and platelet drop requires magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis, BP control with labetalol or hydralazine targeting 140 to 150 systolic, antenatal corticosteroids if under 35 weeks, and planning for delivery. Magpie trial showed magnesium halves eclampsia risk and reduces maternal death. Magnesium toxicity is monitored by reflexes and respiratory rate; calcium gluconate is the antidote.
key_points: Magnesium sulfate loading 4 g IV over 20 min, then 1 g/hr; halves eclampsia risk
BP target around 140 to 150 systolic; not lower
Calcium gluconate is the magnesium toxicity antidote
explanation.pearls: HELLP variant (haemolysis, raised LFTs, low platelets) overlaps with severe pre-eclampsia; LDH over 600 plus haptoglobin reduction confirms haemolysis.

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar called to a multiparous woman whose head has just delivered but the anterior shoulder has not advanced with the next contraction. The turtle sign is observed. The estimated fetal weight at growth scan three weeks ago was 4.1 kg. The midwife has summoned the consultant, the second registrar, and the neonatal team. You are at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 124/76, HR 102; fetal heart rate previously normal
data_table.Examination: Head delivered with turtle sign; anterior shoulder not yet visible at introitus

lead_in: Which manoeuvre is the most appropriate first step after calling for help?

options.text: Fundal pressure with continuous downward traction on the fetal head to disimpact the anterior shoulder from the maternal symphysis pubis
options.rationale: Fundal pressure worsens impaction and increases brachial plexus and uterine rupture risk; it is contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

options.text: Perform McRoberts manoeuvre with hyperflexion of the maternal hips against the abdomen combined with suprapubic pressure
options.rationale: McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure resolves around 50% of shoulder dystocias and is the recommended first-line manoeuvre. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

options.text: Immediate Zavanelli manoeuvre with replacement of the fetal head and progression to category one caesarean section without intermediate manoeuvres
options.rationale: Zavanelli is a last-resort manoeuvre after all standard manoeuvres fail; it is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

options.text: Symphysiotomy with sterile preparation and local anaesthetic infiltration to widen the pelvic outlet for immediate delivery in this critical obstetric emergency
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is essentially never used in high-resource settings; standard manoeuvres are first. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

options.text: Administer intravenous nitroglycerin and abandon any further manoeuvre attempt for two minutes while waiting for the consultant arrival
options.rationale: Nitroglycerin can be used for uterine relaxation in some scenarios but is not first-line for shoulder dystocia. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Statement)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency requiring a structured response. The HELPERR mnemonic guides the sequence: Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter pelvis (internal rotation), Remove posterior arm, Roll patient to all fours. McRoberts and suprapubic pressure resolve most cases. Fundal pressure is contraindicated. Document the manoeuvre sequence, head-to-body interval, and side of impaction.
key_points: McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure first; resolves about half of cases
Fundal pressure is contraindicated due to plexus injury and uterine rupture risks
Document head-to-body interval and which shoulder was anterior
explanation.pearls: RANZCOG recommends head-to-body interval over 5 minutes increases hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy risk substantially; structured timing during the manoeuvres improves documentation and outcomes.

subtopic_detail: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes management
stem: You are the obstetrics registrar reviewing a 28-year-old G3P2 woman at 33 weeks gestation referred with a sudden gush of clear fluid two hours ago followed by ongoing leakage. There are no contractions, no abdominal pain, and fetal movements are present. Speculum confirms pooling in the posterior fornix. The patient is afebrile and has no rupture or trauma history.

data_table.Examination: Soft uterus, no tenderness, no contractions on tocography, CTG reactive, speculum confirms pooling, cervix appears closed and uneffaced
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, CRP 6, high vaginal swab and urine MSU sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Place an emergency cervical cerclage to maintain pregnancy and reduce risk of preterm birth following confirmed rupture of membranes at this gestation
options.rationale: Cerclage after membrane rupture is contraindicated due to infection risk. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Statement)

options.text: Commence immediate induction of labour with vaginal prostaglandins given the gestation has reached the 33-week threshold for expedited delivery in current practice
options.rationale: Immediate induction is not first-line at 33 weeks; expectant management with steroids and antibiotics gives better neonatal outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Statement)

options.text: Discharge home with daily temperature monitoring and outpatient follow-up in two days for review and ongoing assessment of fetal wellbeing
options.rationale: PPROM requires inpatient monitoring for chorioamnionitis; outpatient management is not standard at this gestation. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Statement)

options.text: Administer tocolysis with nifedipine prophylactically to allow steroid completion before planning further antenatal management strategy with senior input
options.rationale: Tocolysis is not recommended in PPROM without active preterm labour; it may delay delivery without benefit. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Statement)

options.text: Admit for inpatient monitoring with antenatal corticosteroids, prophylactic antibiotics, and expectant management with planned delivery around 34 weeks
options.rationale: PPROM at 33 weeks requires admission, steroids, prophylactic antibiotics (erythromycin), and expectant management with delivery planned around 34 to 36 weeks balancing infection versus prematurity risks. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Statement)

explanation.summary: PPROM at 24 to 34 weeks is managed expectantly with antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, prophylactic antibiotics (erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days), magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection if delivery is imminent under 30 weeks, and surveillance for chorioamnionitis. Delivery is planned around 34 to 36 weeks balancing infection versus prematurity. Co-amoxiclav is avoided due to NEC risk in the ORACLE trial.
key_points: Erythromycin (not co-amoxiclav) is the antibiotic of choice in PPROM
Antenatal corticosteroids under 35 weeks; magnesium for neuroprotection under 30 weeks if delivery imminent
Deliver around 34 to 36 weeks balancing prematurity vs chorioamnionitis
explanation.pearls: ORACLE trial demonstrated co-amoxiclav increases necrotising enterocolitis in preterm neonates; erythromycin alone is the standard antibiotic in PPROM.

subtopic_detail: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy recognition
stem: You are the obstetric medicine registrar called to ED to assess a 30-year-old primigravid woman at 35 weeks gestation with three days of nausea, vomiting, and right upper quadrant pain followed by progressive lethargy. She has no past medical history and is on routine prenatal care. She is mildly confused this morning. Her sister was admitted with similar symptoms when pregnant 8 years ago.

data_table.Examination: Mildly jaundiced, mild RUQ tenderness, no peripheral oedema, no asterixis but slow to follow commands, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: ALT 248, AST 312, bilirubin 92 (predominantly conjugated), INR 1.8, ammonia 84, glucose 2.6, urate 0.48, creatinine 124, platelets 138, urine protein 1+, fibrinogen 1.2 g/L

lead_in: Which feature most strongly favours acute fatty liver of pregnancy over HELLP syndrome?

options.text: Onset in the third trimester with right upper quadrant pain and a transaminase elevation similar to a typical HELLP syndrome biochemical profile
options.rationale: Both AFLP and HELLP present in the third trimester with RUQ pain and transaminitis. (Source: SOMANZ Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: The presence of mild hypertension above the booking value in association with bilirubin elevation and right upper quadrant tenderness on examination
options.rationale: Mild hypertension and RUQ pain occur in both conditions and do not discriminate. (Source: SOMANZ Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Mild proteinuria one plus on dipstick and a modest reduction in the maternal platelet count to below normal limits without other systemic features
options.rationale: Mild proteinuria and modest thrombocytopenia overlap with HELLP and do not specifically favour AFLP. (Source: SOMANZ Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Hypoglycaemia with coagulopathy out of proportion to platelet count and the presence of encephalopathy on clinical assessment
options.rationale: AFLP characteristically has hypoglycaemia, marked coagulopathy out of proportion to thrombocytopenia, encephalopathy, and raised ammonia, distinguishing it from HELLP where the platelet drop dominates. (Source: SOMANZ Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: A family history of similar symptoms in a sister during pregnancy with full recovery after expedited delivery and supportive management
options.rationale: Family history may suggest LCHAD deficiency in the fetus but is supportive rather than the strongest discriminator. (Source: SOMANZ Liver Disease in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare third-trimester emergency with maternal mortality 7 to 18%. Swansea criteria help diagnosis (vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia, encephalopathy, raised bilirubin, hypoglycaemia, raised urate, leucocytosis, ascites, raised transaminases, raised ammonia, renal impairment, coagulopathy, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy). Treatment is delivery (regardless of gestation) with supportive care including dextrose for hypoglycaemia, FFP for coagulopathy, and ICU monitoring.
key_points: Hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy out of proportion to platelets, and encephalopathy point to AFLP
LCHAD fetal deficiency is associated; screen newborn after delivery
Definitive treatment is expedited delivery regardless of gestation
explanation.pearls: AFLP fetal LCHAD deficiency (long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase) is identifiable on newborn screening; maternal heterozygosity for LCHAD predisposes to AFLP in affected pregnancies.

sources.label: RCOG Obstetric Cholestasis and Liver Disease in Pregnancy
